decks.description: This is a mono-white +1/+1 counter aggro deck built around Basri Ket and creatures that reward stacking counters. Basri's Lieutenant puts a counter on a creature when it enters and then converts any counter-bearing creature that dies into a 2/2 vigilance Knight token, turning combat trades into value. Basri's Acolyte spreads counters to two other creatures on entry, Tempered Veteran can repeatedly grow anything that already has a counter, and Siege Striker taps down your team to pump itself into a double-striking finisher. Basri Ket himself ties it together: his +1 hands out a counter and indestructible to protect your key creature in combat, his -2 turns a wide attack into a swarm of tapped-and-attacking Soldier tokens, and Basri's Solidarity is a one-shot counters-matter overrun effect that can push through lethal damage. Feat of Resistance and Legion's Judgment give you combat tricks and clean removal, while Faith's Fetters locks down a problem permanent and gains life.
tips: Target Basri's Lieutenant with counters
Use Lieutenant to convert deaths into Knights
Protect key creature with Basri's +1
Alpha strike after Basri's -2
Save Solidarity for lethal push

decks.description: This white deck leans on blink and counter synergies anchored by Emiel the Blessed, who can repeatedly exile and return your own creatures to reset enter-the-battlefield triggers, and whose passive lets you pay {G/W} to drop a +1/+1 counter (two if the creature is a Unicorn) whenever any creature you control enters. Valorous Steed's Knight token and Brightmare's tap-and-drain effect both get extra value when blinked, and Cloudshift offers the same reset for a one-time flicker at instant speed to dodge removal or re-trigger an enters-the-battlefield ability. Blessed Sanctuary turns every nontoken creature you play into a free 2/2 Unicorn token and shields your team from noncombat damage, while combat tricks like Inspired Charge, Inspiring Unicorn's attack trigger, and Valorous Stance's flexible removal/protection help push through combat. The overall plan is to flood the board with tokens and counters, then swing with a buffed wide team.
tips: Blink creatures with Emiel for repeat triggers
Pay G/W off Emiel for extra counters
Flicker with Cloudshift to dodge removal
Play nontoken creatures to trigger Blessed Sanctuary
Swing wide then finish with Inspired Charge

decks.description: A mono-white flyers deck stacked with Angels that wins through evasive damage and lifelink. Baneslayer Angel and Serra Angel are premium flying threats, while Anointed Chorister and its pump ability let it become a late-game lifelink finisher. Celestial Enforcer's tap ability is enabled by the deck's own high flyer count, letting you lock down blockers or attackers as long as you control any flying creature. Angelic Ascension and Angelic Edict serve as flexible removal that exiles problem permanents (Ascension does hand the opponent a 4/4 flying Angel token, so it's best used on your biggest threats), and Scroll of Avacyn synergizes with the Angel tribal theme, converting into a card plus 5 life whenever you control an Angel. The deck otherwise plays a straightforward curve of efficient flying bodies backed by Feat of Resistance for protection and Faith's Fetters for lockdown removal with a lifegain bonus.
tips: Keep a flyer up for Celestial Enforcer
Save Ascension for biggest threats
Sac Scroll of Avacyn while controlling an Angel
Race with lifelink flyers
Protect key creatures with Feat of Resistance

decks.description: A mono-white Angel tribal control-aggro deck that leans on evasive flyers and disruptive locks. Angelic Arbiter creates a brutal tempo lock, punishing opponents for either casting spells or attacking, while Linvala, Keeper of Silence shuts off all opposing activated abilities from creatures, neutralizing decks built around tap abilities or sacrifice engines. Celestial Enforcer's tap-down ability again only works while you control a flyer, which this deck has in spades between Serra Angel, Emancipation Angel, and Angelic Arbiter itself. Angelic Ascension and Angelic Edict provide exile-based removal, and Scroll of Avacyn's card draw and 5 life trigger off any Angel you control. Take Heart rewards a wide attacking board with both a pump and a burst of lifegain, capping off a deck that wants to stabilize behind flyers and lock effects before closing out with evasive damage.
tips: Land Arbiter to lock down opponent
Linvala shuts off activated abilities
Keep a flyer for Celestial Enforcer
Attack wide before casting Take Heart
Exile biggest threats with Angelic Edict

decks.description: A mono-white Dog tribal aggro deck that curves out with cheap bodies and buffs the whole pack via Pack Leader, which gives other Dogs +1/+1 and prevents all combat damage to your Dogs when it attacks, letting you swing in safely. Release the Dogs floods the board with four 1/1 Dog tokens that immediately benefit from Pack Leader's static buff, and Supply Runners' entry trigger puts a +1/+1 counter on every other creature you control, turning a wide token board into a real threat in one shot. Selfless Savior and Cathar's Companion provide protection pieces (indestructible on sacrifice, or indestructible whenever you cast a noncreature spell) to keep your team alive through removal or combat, while Rambunctious Mutt and Trusty Retriever offer artifact/enchantment interaction and value on entry. Pacifism and Secure the Scene round out the removal suite to deal with problem blockers.
tips: Flood board then attack with Pack Leader
Pump tokens with Supply Runners
Use Selfless Savior to save key Dog
Cathar's Companion protects after noncreature spells

decks.description: A near-identical mono-white Dog tribal aggro deck to its counterpart, again centered on Pack Leader's team-wide +1/+1 buff and its attack trigger that prevents all combat damage to your Dogs, letting your pack swing in with no risk of losing creatures. Release the Dogs creates an instant army of four 1/1 tokens that all get boosted by Pack Leader, while Supply Runners' entry ability puts a +1/+1 counter on every other creature you control, turning a board of small Dogs into serious threats. Isamaru, Hound of Konda adds a cheap, efficient 2/2 body to the curve, and Dauntless Onslaught replaces Selfless Savior as a combat trick that pumps two attackers at once rather than granting protection. Trusty Retriever and Rambunctious Mutt again provide flexible value and artifact/enchantment answers, with Pacifism and Secure the Scene handling problem permanents.
tips: Flood board then swing with Pack Leader
Pump tokens via Supply Runners
Use Dauntless Onslaught for double pump

decks.description: A mono-white Auras deck that wants to stack enchantments onto a single evasive or resilient creature to generate huge stat boosts and card advantage. Kor Spiritdancer is the engine: it gets +2/+2 for each Aura attached to it and draws a card every time you cast an Aura spell, meaning cheap Auras like Dub, Face of Divinity, and Indomitable Will both grow the Spiritdancer and refill your hand. Face of Divinity has its own built-in synergy, granting first strike and lifelink to its bearer as long as another Aura is also attached, which is easy to trigger when you're layering multiple Auras on the same body. Blessed Spirits similarly rewards you for casting enchantment spells by growing with a +1/+1 counter each time, and Knightly Valor adds a free 2/2 vigilant Knight token on top of its stat boost. Trusty Retriever can rebuy a lost Aura from the graveyard, and Faith's Fetters/Forced Worship provide lockdown removal for problem creatures.
tips: Stack Auras on Kor Spiritdancer
Draw cards off Spiritdancer's trigger
Combo Face of Divinity with second Aura

decks.description: Another mono-white enchantments deck, this one built around Ajani's Chosen turning every enchantment you cast into a free 2/2 Cat token (and letting you attach Auras straight onto that new token), which combines especially well with Faith's Fetters, Forced Worship, Celestial Mantle, Face of Divinity, and Knightly Valor to generate bodies alongside removal or buffs. Knightly Valor doubles up on this plan by creating its own 2/2 Knight token in addition to the Cat from Ajani's Chosen, so a single Aura cast can net you two extra creatures plus a buffed target. Blessed Spirits again grows on enchantment spells, and Celestial Mantle's combat damage trigger doubles your life total, a huge swing if it connects even once. Stone Haven Pilgrim gets bigger and gains lifelink whenever it attacks as long as you control any artifact or enchantment, which this deck does easily. Trusty Retriever and Bulwark Giant round out the curve with value and lifegain.

decks.description: A mono-white lifegain deck that converts life totals into board presence and card advantage. Speaker of the Heavens can tap to create a 4/4 flying Angel token for free once you're 7 life above your starting total, which is easy to hit thanks to Basri's Acolyte, Bulwark Giant, Brightmare, and Revitalize all padding your life total early. Griffin Aerie rewards any turn you gain 3 or more life with a free 2/2 flying Griffin token, meaning cards like Revitalize (gain 3, draw a card) or a lifelink hit from Mesa Unicorn or Anointed Chorister can passively generate an army over time. Light of Promise turns all that incidental lifegain into direct stat growth by putting a +1/+1 counter on the enchanted creature equal to life gained, so enchanting an early lifelinker turns every attack into a snowballing threat. Faith's Fetters and Swift Response provide removal, with Take Heart giving a late-game pump plus lifegain finisher.

Enchant a lifelinker with Light of Promise

decks.description: A mono-white lifegain value deck almost identical in structure to its counterpart, again built to pad your life total and convert that into tokens and growing threats. Griffin Aerie hands out a free 2/2 flying Griffin whenever you gain 3 or more life in a turn, which is trivially enabled by Angel of Mercy, Basri's Acolyte, Brightmare, and Revitalize's built-in lifegain-plus-draw. Speaker of the Heavens turns excess life into an even bigger 4/4 flying Angel token once you're 7 life ahead, and Light of Promise converts any lifegain on its enchanted creature directly into +1/+1 counters, so putting it on a lifelinker like Anointed Chorister or Stone Haven Pilgrim creates a snowballing attacker. Moment of Heroism adds a combat trick that grants lifelink for a turn, feeding both Griffin Aerie and Light of Promise simultaneously if the enchanted or targeted creature connects. Secure the Scene and Swift Response give flexible removal to buy time for the lifegain plan to take over.

Enchant a lifelinker with Light of Promise
Use Moment of Heroism to double-dip lifegain

decks.description: This mono-white lifegain deck wants to trigger 'gain 3+ life this turn' as often as possible to fuel Griffin Aerie, which spits out a 2/2 flying Griffin token at end step whenever that threshold is hit. Nearly every card contributes life gain: Angel of Mercy, Basri's Acolyte, Brightmare, Faith's Fetters, Moment of Heroism, and Revitalize all pad your total, while a suite of Lifelink bodies (Anointed Chorister, Mesa Unicorn, Rhox Faithmender) keep triggering it through combat as well. Rhox Faithmender doubles all life gained, which turns even small triggers like Revitalize's 3 life or Angel of Mercy's ETB into much bigger swings and makes hitting the Griffin Aerie threshold trivial. Light of Promise turns all that incidental life gain into permanent +1/+1 counters on a chosen creature, letting one threat grow enormous over a long game. The deck isn't fast, but it grinds out value and stabilizes with lifelink blockers while accumulating Griffin tokens and a scaling threat.

decks.description: Similar to its sibling lifegain builds, this deck chains life-gain triggers to fuel Griffin Aerie's end-step Griffin tokens and Cradle of Vitality's counter-generation, while Path of Bravery rewards staying above starting life with a team-wide +1/+1 buff and extra life whenever you attack with multiple creatures. Angel of Mercy, Basri's Acolyte, Bulwark Giant, and Revitalize all provide big one-time life bursts, and Cradle of Vitality lets you convert any life gain into permanent +1/+1 counters by paying {1}{W}, so a Bulwark Giant's 5 life becomes five counters spread as you choose. Alabaster Mage can grant lifelink to any creature on demand, extending the life-gain engine into combat even on creatures that don't naturally have it. The overall plan is grindy: stack incidental life gain to keep Path of Bravery's bonus active, snowball board state with Cradle of Vitality counters, and close games with buffed attackers and Griffin tokens.

decks.description: A white flying-tribal aggro deck that wants a critical mass of fliers on board to power up its payoffs. Steel-Plume Marshal pumps all other attacking fliers by +2/+2 when it attacks, turning a swarm of small flying bodies like Healer's Hawk and Concordia Pegasus into lethal threats in one combat step. Aven Gagglemaster rewards you retroactively for already having fliers out, gaining 2 life per flying creature when it enters, and Falconer Adept adds another flying body to the team every time it attacks. Celestial Enforcer's tap ability is gated behind controlling a flier, giving you repeatable removal-lite once the board is established. Combat tricks like Dauntless Onslaught and Moment of Heroism, plus Sky Tether to ground down enemy fliers or big blockers, round out a deck that wants to go over the top in the air and finish with a big alpha strike once Steel-Plume Marshal is online.
tips: Build a flier board before attacking with Marshal
Steel-Plume Marshal pump wins combats
Time Aven Gagglemaster after board fills

decks.description: This flying-tribal deck again centers on Steel-Plume Marshal's attack trigger giving all other attacking fliers +2/+2, making even a small squadron of Healer's Hawk, Concordia Pegasus, and Falconer Adept's Bird tokens deal huge combat damage in a single swing. Aven Gagglemaster gains 2 life per flier already in play when it lands, rewarding you for committing to the air early, while Celestial Enforcer's tap ability again requires a flier to activate. This build leans more removal-oriented with Angelic Ascension, which permanently exiles a problem creature or planeswalker (at the cost of gifting a 4/4 flying Angel token to the opponent) and Swift Response to snipe tapped blockers. Tandem Tactics offers a flexible combat trick that pumps two creatures and gains life, smoothing out combat math while keeping the life total safe. Overall it's an aggressive flier swarm that uses targeted removal to clear the way for a Marshal-boosted alpha strike.

decks.description: This flier-focused white deck mixes the usual go-wide air force (Concordia Pegasus, Healer's Hawk, Falconer Adept's Bird tokens, Aven Gagglemaster) with Angel of the Dire Hour, a Flash flier that can ambush an alpha strike by exiling all attacking creatures when cast from hand during combat, turning a defensive moment into a blowout. Aven Gagglemaster's lifegain-per-flier ETB rewards building a wide board first, and Celestial Enforcer's repeatable tap-down ability only works once you have a flier in play. Combat tricks like Dauntless Onslaught and Take Heart (which gains life per attacker) help push through combat math, while Sky Tether shuts down an opposing flier or big threat by grounding it with defender. The deck's plan is to develop a flier board, use Angel of the Dire Hour as a surprise board wipe against a wide opposing attack, and then close with tricked-out flying damage.

decks.description: This version of the flying deck emphasizes value and inevitability over raw speed. Archon of Redemption gains life equal to power whenever it or another flier enters, rewarding continual flier deployment, while Archon of Justice punishes opponents on death by exiling any permanent, making it a strong trade even into removal. Falconer Adept keeps generating extra 1/1 flying Bird tokens on attack to feed both Archons' synergies and provide combat math advantages, and Celestial Enforcer again needs a flier present to repeatedly tap down blockers or attackers. Aegis of the Heavens is a surprise combat trick that gives a creature +1/+7, making it nearly unkillable in combat and able to survive a big trade or removal spell, while Sky Tether locks down problem fliers or big ground threats. The deck plays a longer game than the more aggressive Feathered Friends builds, using Archons for card advantage and resilience rather than a pure alpha-strike plan.

decks.description: A white +1/+1 counters deck built around Cathars' Crusade, which puts a counter on every creature you control whenever any creature enters — including itself triggering off Basri's Acolyte, Patron of the Valiant, or Trusty Retriever coming down, snowballing the whole board bigger with each new creature. Patron of the Valiant doubles down by giving a counter to every creature that already has one the moment it enters, and Lightwalker turns any counter into evasion by granting itself flying whenever it holds one. Siege Striker and Makeshift Battalion both reward wide attacks — Siege Striker can tap down other creatures to pump itself, while Makeshift Battalion grows permanently once three or more creatures swing together. Feat of Resistance and Basri's Solidarity protect the board or blanket-buff it further, while Secure the Scene handles problem permanents. The deck's core plan is to keep deploying creatures to trigger Cathars' Crusade repeatedly, turning a modest board into an overwhelming army of counter-laden threats.

decks.description: This +1/+1 counters deck again revolves around Cathars' Crusade, distributing a counter to every creature you control each time a new one enters, which combos especially well with Basri's Acolyte and Trusty Retriever's ETB triggers and Patron of the Valiant's ability to double up on creatures that already carry counters. Lightwalker becomes an evasive threat the instant it picks up a single counter, while Tempered Veteran offers a repeatable mana sink to keep piling counters onto creatures that already have them, ensuring the strongest bodies keep growing. Battlefield Promotion and Gird for Battle are both efficient ways to seed early counters and stabilize with life gain or first strike, setting up bigger Cathars' Crusade triggers later, while Siege Striker and Makeshift Battalion reward committing to wide attacks. Secure the Scene provides flexible removal for anything threatening to derail the counters plan. Overall this is a snowballing go-wide deck that wants to keep adding creatures to the board to maximize Cathars' Crusade value turn after turn.

decks.description: This counters-matter build swaps in Mikaeus, the Lunarch and Supply Runners as extra engines alongside the usual Basri's Acolyte, Patron of the Valiant, and Trusty Retriever counter-spreaders. Supply Runners immediately puts a counter on every other creature the moment it enters, functioning like a one-shot Cathars' Crusade effect, while Mikaeus can tap to grow himself or remove one of his own counters to spread a counter to every other creature, giving you a repeatable, mana-free way to buff the team turn after turn. Patron of the Valiant then doubles up on any creature that already has a counter, and Lightwalker converts a single counter into evasive flying damage. Battlefield Promotion and Basri's Solidarity provide more ways to blanket the board with counters and stabilize with life gain or first strike, while Divine Arrow offers cheap, efficient removal against attackers or blockers. The deck plays a patient counters-snowball game, using Mikaeus as a recurring value engine rather than relying purely on ETB triggers.

decks.description: This counters build features High Sentinels of Arashin, a flier that grows permanently based on how many other creatures you control with a counter, making it a scaling finisher fed by Supply Runners' one-time counter-spread on ETB and Basri's Acolyte's targeted counters. Tempered Veteran offers a repeatable way to keep adding counters to creatures that already have one, directly buffing High Sentinels further, while Lightwalker again turns a single counter into flying evasion. Long Road Home offers flexible removal that exiles a creature and returns it with a bonus counter if it's your own creature under threat, and Duelist's Heritage can grant double strike to any attacker, which is especially deadly on a counter-loaded Siege Striker or Makeshift Battalion body. Swift Response handles tapped blockers cleanly. This build plays a longer counters-value game, aiming to build a wide, counter-laden board that culminates in a massive High Sentinels of Arashin backed by double strike finishers.

decks.description: This is a white go-wide aggro deck built around flooding the board with cheap tokens and then pumping the whole team at once. Raise the Alarm and Falconer Adept's attack trigger add extra bodies, while Valorous Steed's Knight token and Blessed Sanctuary's Unicorn tokens (triggered whenever a nontoken creature enters) keep reinforcements coming even after the opening rush. The payoff is Basri's Solidarity, which drops a +1/+1 counter on every creature you control, and Inspired Charge, which gives the whole team +2/+1 for a turn - both are much bigger blowouts when you've already got five or six tokens on board. Makeshift Battalion's battalion ability rewards attacking with three or more creatures by growing itself permanently, reinforcing the deck's incentive to swing wide rather than trade one-for-one. Selfless Savior and Path to Exile provide small amounts of protection and removal to keep the plan alive against bigger threats.

decks.description: A white token-swarm aggro deck that wants to make several small creatures early (Raise the Alarm, Release the Dogs is absent here but Valorous Steed's Knight token still helps) and then buff them all simultaneously with Glorious Anthem, Basri's Solidarity, and Inspiring Captain's one-turn +1/+1. Siege Striker is the deck's key payoff: its attack trigger lets you tap any number of untapped creatures to add that many +1/+1 counters worth of stats to itself until end of turn, meaning a board full of tokens can be converted into a single enormous double striker that hits for absurd damage. Makeshift Battalion rewards attacking with three or more creatures, and Faith's Fetters and Legion's Judgment provide removal for the bigger threats that could otherwise stabilize against the swarm. The game plan is straightforward: build a wide board, then use combat tricks and anthems to close it out in one or two big attack steps.

decks.description: This white aggro deck leans on token generation from Raise the Alarm, Release the Dogs, and Valorous Steed's Knight token to quickly fill the board, then leverages anthem effects like Glorious Anthem and Basri's Solidarity to turn that width into real damage. The standout synergy is Siege Striker, which can tap any number of your other untapped creatures when it attacks to gain +1/+1 for each one tapped, effectively converting a wide board of small tokens into one massive double-striking finisher. Makeshift Battalion also grows permanently whenever it attacks alongside two or more other creatures, so the deck wants to commit multiple creatures to combat rather than trickle them in one at a time. Faith's Fetters and Legion's Judgment offer situational removal to buy time for the go-wide plan to come online, while Fortify is a flexible combat trick that can push through extra damage or protect the team from a sweeper or blocks.

decks.description: A white go-wide token deck that adds a card-advantage engine on top of the usual token-and-anthem plan. Mentor of the Meek lets you draw a card for {1} whenever another creature with power 2 or less enters, which triggers repeatedly off Raise the Alarm's Soldiers, Valorous Steed's Knight token, and Lena's own token-making. Siege Striker again converts a wide board into a huge double striker by tapping other creatures for +1/+1 each, and Makeshift Battalion, Fortify, and Basri's Solidarity round out the ways this deck turns a crowded board into lethal damage.

decks.description: This is a blue tempo-control deck built around card draw and time manipulation, centered on Teferi, Master of Time. His +1 loops draw-discard every turn and can be activated at instant speed on any player's turn, letting you hold up interaction while still filtering your hand, and his -3 phases out an opposing threat as a pseudo-removal spell. Tolarian Kraken turns every card draw into a mana-efficient tap or untap effect, meaning Teferi's +1, Opt, Exclude, and Leave in the Dust all double as ways to tap down blockers or untap your own creatures. Teferi's Ageless Insight doubles your draws beyond the first each turn, which massively accelerates both Tolarian Kraken triggers and Teferi's Tutelage's mill clock, since Tutelage mills the opponent two cards every time you draw a card. The deck's win conditions are Talrand's Invocation's flying Drakes and grinding out card advantage until Teferi's Tutelage or ultimate closes the game.

decks.description: This deck's win condition is entirely about milling the opponent out rather than attacking with creatures. Bruvac the Grandiloquent doubles every mill effect against opponents, so Wall of Lost Thoughts' four-card mill becomes eight, Vedalken Entrancer's two-mana mill-two becomes four, and Thought Scour and Thought Collapse's mill effects likewise double, making Bruvac the centerpiece the whole deck wants to protect and build around. Teferi's Tutelage compounds this further by milling the opponent two cards (four with Bruvac) every single time you draw a card, and Reckless Scholar's repeatable loot effect combined with Thought Scour's cantrip mill keeps feeding that trigger. Belltower Sphinx and Towering-Wave Mystic punish opponents who try to fight through combat by milling them for damage dealt, while Selhoff Occultist mills on any creature death, giving the deck redundant mill triggers even outside of combat. Sweep Away and Capture Sphere serve as tempo tools to buy time for the mill plan to finish the opponent's library.

decks.description: This blue Pirates deck generates a stream of Treasure tokens from Corsair Captain, Prosperous Pirates, and Sailor of Means, which smooth out the mana curve and can be sacrificed for mana of any color to power out threats early or ambush with flash creatures like Spectral Sailor. Corsair Captain's static ability pumps every other Pirate by +1/+1, turning cheap creatures like Kitesail Corsair (which only flies while attacking) and Rishadan Airship into real threats, while Pirate's Cutlass automatically attaches to a Pirate for another +2/+1 boost. Coastal Piracy and Chart a Course reward attacking by drawing extra cards whenever combat damage connects, and Departed Deckhand's near-unblockability (only blockable by Spirits) combined with its ability to make another creature similarly evasive helps push that damage through consistently. Waterknot and Voyage's End are cheap tempo tools to remove blockers so the Pirate crew can keep attacking unimpeded.

Corsair Captain's anthem effect makes every other Pirate bigger, and Pirate's Cutlass adds another +2/+1 on entry, turning evasive creatures like Kitesail Corsair and Rishadan Airship into real clock threats. Capture Sphere provides cheap flash removal to clear a blocker right before combat, letting the Pirate crew keep attacking through and refilling the hand via Curious Obsession and Read the Tides.

decks.description: This blue Spirits tempo deck is built to tap down opposing blockers and bounce threats while chipping in with evasive fliers. Nebelgast Herald taps a target creature every time it or another Spirit enters the battlefield, and Shacklegeist can tap two untapped Spirits to tap down another creature on demand, so the deck consistently shuts off blockers before combat. Battleground Geist buffs every other Spirit's power by +1/+0, turning the crew of small fliers into a real clock, while Roaming Ghostlight's ETB bounces a non-Spirit creature for tempo. Essence Flux is a key synergy piece: flickering one of your own Spirit creatures re-triggers its enter-the-battlefield ability (like Nebelgast Herald's tap effect) and also drops a +1/+1 counter on it since it's a Spirit. Tome Anima rewards the deck's card-draw spells like Winged Words and Befuddle by becoming unblockable once you've drawn two or more cards in a turn.
tips: Nebelgast Herald taps down blockers
Essence Flux re-triggers Spirit ETBs
Shacklegeist taps threats on demand
Draw 2+ cards to unblock Tome Anima
Battleground Geist buffs whole team

decks.description: This blue Spirits deck focuses on tapping down blockers and protecting its evasive fliers to close games through the air. Nebelgast Herald taps a target creature whenever it or another Spirit enters, and Shacklegeist can tap two Spirits to tap another creature, keeping the opponent's board locked down during combat. Rattlechains is the deck's key value piece: its flash and flying let it ambush at instant speed, its ETB grants a Spirit hexproof to protect your best threat from removal, and it lets you cast your other Spirit spells (like Nebelgast Herald or Roaming Ghostlight) with flash for maximum surprise value and mana efficiency. Battleground Geist pumps the rest of the Spirit crew, Roaming Ghostlight bounces problematic non-Spirit blockers, and Rewind protects the board by countering a spell while untapping four lands to hold up more interaction. Rookie Mistake and Winged Words give small tempo and card-draw boosts to keep the flying beatdown going.
tips: Rattlechains grants Spirit flash-casting
Hexproof your key threat
Nebelgast Herald taps blockers pre-combat
Rewind protects while refunding mana
Shacklegeist locks down attackers

decks.description: This is a mono-blue tempo-control deck built around bouncing and locking down opposing creatures while slowly generating card advantage. Waterknot and Voyage's End handle threats by tapping them down permanently or bouncing them back to hand, while scry effects from Octoprophet and Sigiled Starfish smooth draws. Pursued Whale is a real two-for-one: it forces the opponent into a 1/1 Pirate token that can't block and must attack, while also taxing removal aimed at the Whale itself by {3}, making it awkward for the opponent to profitably deal with. Tolarian Kraken turns every card draw into a way to tap down a blocker or untap your own attacker, synergizing with the deck's card-draw pieces like Rousing Read (which draws two, discards one, and gives flying) to generate repeated tempo swings. Waker of Waves shrinks the opposing board's power while your Kraken and Whale close out the game in the air or over a stalled ground.
tips: Waterknot problem creatures early
Chain scry effects for smooth draws
Use Kraken's draw trigger to tap blockers
Let Pursued Whale force bad attacks
Hold Voyage's End for tempo swings

decks.description: A mono-blue control shell that leans harder into mass bounce and graveyard-fueled cost reduction than its counterpart. Whelming Wave is the centerpiece: it returns every creature to hand except your own Krakens, Leviathans, Octopuses, and Serpents, which conveniently includes your own Cryptic Serpent and Tolarian-style sea monsters, letting you wipe the board of opposing blockers/attackers while keeping your finishers on the battlefield. Cryptic Serpent gets cheaper as instants and sorceries pile into your graveyard, so bounce spells like Sweep Away and Waterknot effectively pay for a big creature later. Pursued Whale again punishes attackers into a forced-attack Pirate token while taxing removal, and scry/looting effects (Octoprophet, Sigiled Starfish, Waker of Waves' discard ability) keep your hand full of answers as you grind toward Whelming Wave.
tips: Save Whelming Wave for board wipes
Fill graveyard for cheap Cryptic Serpent
Keep sea monsters on board pre-Wave
Use Sweep Away on attackers
Waterknot annoying threats

decks.description: This deck is a classic Azorius-colored (mostly blue with a white splash via Inniaz) flying-tempo strategy that wants to fill the skies and push through evasive damage while backing it up with tempo spells. Tide Skimmer rewards attacking with two or more fliers by drawing a card each combat, and Warden of Evos Isle discounts your flying creature spells, letting you curve out fliers faster. Inniaz, the Gale Force is the top-end payoff: once you get three or more flying attackers in, you steal a permanent from each opponent, and her static ability also pumps all attacking fliers. Lofty Denial gets much harder to beat once you have any flier in play, effectively becoming a near-hard counter, while Capture Sphere and Unsubstantiate handle problem blockers so your fliers connect unmolested.
tips: Curve fliers with Warden discount
Attack wide in the air for Tide Skimmer draws
Push for 3 fliers to trigger Inniaz
Hold Lofty Denial once fliers are out
Capture Sphere clears blockers

decks.description: Another Azorius-flavored flying deck, this build swaps some of the discount pieces for extra card advantage and instant-speed disruption. Tide Skimmer still rewards attacking with multiple fliers by drawing cards, and Talrand's Invocation instantly creates two 2/2 flying Drakes to fill out the attacking team needed for Inniaz's ability, which steals a permanent from each opponent once three or more of your fliers attack together. Frost Breath and Capture Sphere function as tempo removal to clear blockers before combat, while Lofty Denial scales into a much harder counter as soon as you have a flier in play. Rain of Revelation refuels your hand after you've dumped your low curve onto the board, keeping the aggression going into the mid-game.
tips: Talrand's Invocation fills the skies fast
Attack with 3+ fliers for Inniaz theft
Frost Breath to clear blockers pre-combat
Lofty Denial punishes removal
Refill with Rain of Revelation

decks.description: This flyers deck focuses even more on card advantage from attacking in the air, stacking multiple triggers that reward evasive attacks. Both Tide Skimmer and Windreader Sphinx let you draw cards whenever fliers attack, so once you have a small air force established you can out-card the opponent every combat step. Warden of Evos Isle discounts flying creature spells to help you deploy that air force quickly, and Serendib Efreet provides an efficient, aggressive flier (at the cost of 1 life per upkeep) to pressure early. Frost Breath and Capture Sphere clear the way for attacks, and Lofty Denial becomes a much tougher counterspell to beat as soon as any flier is on board, protecting your board state as you grind card advantage into a win.
tips: Stack Tide Skimmer + Windreader Sphinx draws
Discount fliers with Warden of Evos Isle
Race with Serendib Efreet early
Frost Breath to open attacks
Lofty Denial once a flier resolves

decks.description: This build of the flying archetype adds a protective layer to the usual evasive attack plan. Kira, Great Glass-Spinner gives every creature you control a built-in Spellskite-like protection, countering the first spell or ability that targets each of your creatures each turn, which shields your key fliers like Tide Skimmer and Windstorm Drake from opposing removal or bounce. Windstorm Drake pumps all your other fliers, and Tide Skimmer converts multi-flier attacks into card draw, letting the deck snowball once it establishes a couple of evasive threats. Rookie Mistake and Capture Sphere handle blockers, while Lofty Denial again scales into a hard counter with any flier in play, rounding out a deck that wants to protect its air force and keep attacking unimpeded.
tips: Kira protects key fliers from targeting
Windstorm Drake pumps the squad
Attack with 2+ fliers for card draw
Rookie Mistake clears a blocker
Lofty Denial with any flier out

decks.description: This is a mono-blue artifact value deck whose engine revolves around recurring artifacts from the graveyard for repeated value. Archaeomender's enters-the-battlefield trigger returns an artifact card from your graveyard to hand, and this pairs directly with sacrifice-based artifacts like Aether Spellbomb and Chromatic Sphere (which sacrifice themselves for value) and Buried Ruin (a land that sacrifices to fetch an artifact from the graveyard back to hand), letting you loop utility artifacts for repeated bounces, draws, or ramp. Scholar of the Lost Trove is the other major payoff, letting you recast an instant, sorcery, or artifact straight from the graveyard for free when it enters, potentially replaying a Thirst for Knowledge or reusing a spellbomb effect. Ancestral Statue lets you return one of your own permanents to hand, which can rebuy an Archaeomender or another ETB creature for more value, while Juggernaut and Meteor Golem provide the beefy artifact bodies that close games once the value engine has ground out card and board advantage.
tips: Loop artifacts with Archaeomender + Buried Ruin
Bounce your own ETBs with Ancestral Statue
Scholar of the Lost Trove rebuys spells
Sac spellbombs for value before they die
Meteor Golem as removal on a body

decks.description: A variant of the mono-blue artifact recursion deck that trades some card selection for bigger ramp payoffs. The core loop remains: Archaeomender returns artifacts from graveyard to hand, feeding off cheap sacrifice artifacts like Aether Spellbomb and Terrarion, the latter of which also draws a card the moment it dies, whether sacrificed for mana or otherwise, making it a natural target to rebuy with Archaeomender or Buried Ruin. Dreamstone Hedron and Terrarion ramp you into your bigger threats like Meteor Golem (which destroys a nonland permanent on entry) and Juggernaut, while Scholar of the Lost Trove flashes back an instant, sorcery, or artifact spell from the graveyard for free. Ancestral Statue's bounce lets you replay Archaeomender or another ETB creature to keep the recursion train running, grinding out incremental advantage until the artifact threats take over the game.
tips: Rebuy Terrarion for its death draw
Ramp with Dreamstone Hedron into Golem
Loop Archaeomender via Buried Ruin
Bounce ETBs with Ancestral Statue
Scholar of the Lost Trove for free spells

decks.description: This build of the artifact deck shifts toward an artifact-typal payoff plan rather than pure recursion. Vedalken Archmage draws a card every time you cast an artifact spell, turning your dense artifact count (Hedron Archive, Aether Spellbomb, Prismite, plus artifact creatures) into a steady card-draw engine, while Sharding Sphinx creates a 1/1 flying Thopter token whenever an artifact creature you control connects with a player, letting Juggernaut's forced attacks or Meteor Golem's body generate extra bodies over time. Archaeomender still recurs artifacts from the graveyard, which combines with Buried Ruin's similar ability to keep feeding Vedalken Archmage more artifact spells to recast, and Meteor Golem clears the way for those combat triggers by destroying a blocker on entry. The overall plan is to flood the board with cheap artifacts, draw extra cards off Archmage, and slowly accumulate Thopters from Sharding Sphinx to overwhelm the opponent.
tips: Vedalken Archmage rewards artifact density
Sharding Sphinx makes Thopters off combat damage
Recur artifacts for repeat Archmage triggers
Meteor Golem clears blockers pre-combat
Let Juggernaut force through Sphinx triggers

decks.description: This version of the artifact deck focuses on colorless fixing and reanimation to keep recasting its artifact permanents. Scuttlemutt and Signpost Scarecrow both fix mana of any color while providing bodies, and Scarecrone offers a two-way artifact-recursion engine: sacrifice a Scarecrow to draw a card, or later pay to return an artifact creature card from the graveyard straight to the battlefield, effectively reanimating a Juggernaut or Meteor Golem for a second value trigger. Archaeomender again recurs artifacts from the graveyard to hand, working alongside Buried Ruin to feed Scarecrone's sacrifice ability or simply rebuy Aether Spellbomb for a bounce/draw. Short Sword adds a small equipment buff to close games once the artifact value train has generated enough card and board advantage, with Meteor Golem's removal-on-a-stick smoothing the path to victory.
tips: Reanimate Meteor Golem with Scarecrone
Sac Scarecrows for cards
Loop Archaeomender via Buried Ruin
Equip Short Sword to close games
Fix mana with Scuttlemutt/Signpost Scarecrow

decks.description: This mono-blue deck is built entirely around the 'draw a card' trigger, turning card draw itself into a payoff engine rather than just card advantage. Oneirophage grows a +1/+1 counter every single time you draw, so cheap cantrips like Opt and repeatable draw from Erratic Visionary quickly turn it into a real threat. Tolarian Kraken taps down blockers or untaps your own creatures every time you draw, giving you tempo and evasion for your growing team, while Tome Anima becomes unblockable once you've drawn two cards in a turn, which is trivial with Rousing Read and Curiosity feeding extra cards. Ormos, Archive Keeper is both a card-draw payoff and a late-game mana sink, discarding three uniquely named cards to draw five, and if you ever deck yourself it just piles five counters onto itself instead of losing you the game. Runed Servitor and Suspicious Bookcase round out the curve, giving you a defensive body and unblockable enabler. The deck's plan is simply to chain together draw triggers every turn to snowball Oneirophage and Tome Anima into unblockable, oversized attackers while Narcolepsy and Curiosity lock down or punish the opponent's best blocker.
tips: Chain cantrips into Oneirophage
Draw twice before attacking with Tome Anima
Use Ormos as late-game mana sink
Narcolepsy their best blocker
Curiosity on an evasive creature

decks.description: Nearly identical to its sibling build, this mono-blue deck leans on the same draw-triggers-matter core: Oneirophage grows with every card drawn, Tome Anima becomes unblockable once you've drawn two cards in a turn, and Tolarian Kraken taps or untaps a creature whenever you draw. Here Arcane Encyclopedia adds a repeatable no-questions-asked draw engine to keep those triggers coming every turn, while Capture Sphere gives you a flash removal option that can ambush an attacker or lock down a blocker at instant speed. Rousing Read and Curiosity again supply extra draws and evasion, letting your Oneirophage and Tome Anima snowball into real damage sources. Ormos, Archive Keeper remains the top-end payoff, converting excess cards (or an empty library) directly into a huge flying threat. Overall this is the same 'draw matters' engine deck, just trading some of the softer disruption (Narcolepsy, Suspicious Bookcase) for a steady artifact draw engine and flash removal.
tips: Activate Arcane Encyclopedia every turn
Grow Oneirophage off every draw
Flash in Capture Sphere as a trick
Attack with Tome Anima after two draws
Save Ormos discard ability for late game

decks.description: This version of the draw-matters deck adds Rhystic Study and Cloudreader Sphinx to the usual Oneirophage/Tome Anima/Tolarian Kraken core, giving it even more inevitability. Rhystic Study taxes opponents on every spell they cast, and most opponents in limited will simply let you draw, which keeps feeding Oneirophage's +1/+1 counters and unlocks Tome Anima's unblockable clause. Cloudreader Sphinx adds a solid flying body with a scry 2 to smooth draws, while Suspicious Bookcase and Narcolepsy provide utility to push through damage or shut down a key blocker. The overall plan is unchanged: draw as many cards as possible each turn to snowball your growing threats and make them unblockable, using Curiosity to loop extra cards off combat damage once you land an evasive threat.
tips: Play Rhystic Study early
Let draws snowball Oneirophage
Attack with unblockable Tome Anima
Narcolepsy locks down blockers
Curiosity on your evasive threat

decks.description: This build keeps the same Oneirophage/Tome Anima/Tolarian Kraken draw-payoff shell but adds Mystic Archaeologist and Read the Runes as big late-game draw spikes, plus Capture Sphere for tempo removal. Mystic Archaeologist's repeatable {3}{U}{U} ability and Read the Runes' scalable card draw both let you refuel in one go, immediately triggering Oneirophage's counters and unlocking Tome Anima's unblockable ability for that turn. Cloudreader Sphinx adds an evasive flying body with scry 2 to smooth your draws, and Curiosity keeps the card advantage rolling once an evasive creature connects. Capture Sphere is your interaction, flashing in to neutralize an attacker or a key blocker at instant speed. As with the rest of the cycle, the deck wants to draw as many cards as possible each turn to grow its threats and force through damage.
tips: Big draw turns pump Oneirophage
Read the Runes for scalable card advantage
Attack with unblockable Tome Anima after two draws
Flash Capture Sphere on threats
Curiosity to keep cards flowing

decks.description: This mono-blue Wizards/spells deck is built around Talrand, Sky Summoner, who turns every instant or sorcery you cast into a free 2/2 flying Drake, and Talrand's Invocation itself already makes two Drakes on a stick. Vodalian Arcanist ramps you into spells with mana that can only be spent on instants/sorceries, letting you chain Opt, Winged Words, Befuddle and Frost Breath together to flood the board with flying tokens while bouncing or tapping down opposing blockers. Wizard's Retort discounts down to a one-mana counterspell once you have any Wizard in play (which is most of your creatures), letting you protect Talrand or your tempo plays, and Shipwreck Dowser recurs your best spent instant/sorcery from the graveyard for even more value. The overall gameplan is to control the board with bounce and tempo spells (Exclusion Mage, Befuddle, Frost Breath) while quietly accumulating an army of evasive Drake tokens that eventually close out the game.
tips: Land Talrand before spell slinging
Chain cheap spells for Drakes
Wizard's Retort discount from any Wizard
Bounce blockers with Exclusion Mage
Recur spells with Shipwreck Dowser

decks.description: This Wizards deck trades Talrand for extra tempo-oriented ETB creatures like Crookclaw Transmuter, which can flash in to swap a blocker's power and toughness to blow out combat, and Sea Gate Oracle, which digs for your next play. Vodalian Arcanist still ramps you into your instants and sorceries, and Wizard's Retort remains a cheap counterspell whenever you control any Wizard, which is most of the deck. Riptide Laboratory lets you bounce your own Wizards (like Shipwreck Dowser or Sea Gate Oracle) back to hand to reset and reuse their enter-the-battlefield triggers, effectively looping value off Shipwreck Dowser's instant/sorcery recursion or Sea Gate Oracle's card selection. Talrand's Invocation still supplies a pair of flying Drakes as a go-wide finisher, while Rookie Mistake and Read the Tides provide flexible combat tricks and card draw or bounce. This is a tempo-value deck that grinds out advantage through repeatable ETB effects and cheap countermagic rather than a pure token swarm.
tips: Bounce Wizards with Riptide Laboratory to reuse ETBs
Flash Crookclaw Transmuter to blowout combat
Wizard's Retort cheap with any Wizard
Sea Gate Oracle for card selection
Talrand's Invocation as flier finisher

decks.description: This build again centers on Talrand, Sky Summoner turning every instant and sorcery into a 2/2 flying Drake, backed by Teferi's Protege for repeatable card filtering and Vodalian Arcanist's spell-only mana ramp. Wizard's Retort becomes a one-mana counterspell whenever any Wizard is on the battlefield, and Unsubstantiate offers flexible interaction, bouncing either a problematic creature or countering a spell outright. Capture Sphere adds flash-speed lockdown for an opposing threat, while Read the Tides and Winged Words keep the hand stocked with more spells to feed Talrand. Shipwreck Dowser recurs your best-used instant or sorcery, extending your ability to keep making Drakes turn after turn. This deck's plan is to protect Talrand, keep casting cheap spells to build a flying army, and use its countermagic and bounce to control the pace until the Drakes take over.
tips: Protect Talrand with counters
Chain spells for Drake tokens
Wizard's Retort cheap off any Wizard
Recur spells with Shipwreck Dowser
Unsubstantiate as flexible answer

decks.description: This build swaps Talrand for a suite of bounce-based value engines centered on Barrin, Tolarian Archmage, whose enters-the-battlefield bounce and end-step card draw reward you for repeatedly returning permanents to hand. Storm Sculptor's unblockable body comes with a built-in bounce of one of your own creatures, letting you replay ETB creatures like Sage's Row Savant or Shipwreck Dowser for extra scry or spell recursion, and directly triggers Barrin's end-step draw if it bounces a creature that turn. Peel from Reality doubles as removal and tempo, bouncing an opposing threat while resetting one of your own ETB creatures again. Rain of Revelation and Read the Tides refill your hand, while Teferi's Protege loots away excess lands, and Wizard's Retort remains a cheap counter whenever you control a Wizard. The deck's core loop is bouncing your own value creatures with Storm Sculptor or Barrin to re-trigger their ETBs while also drawing a card off Barrin's ability, grinding out card advantage rather than going wide with tokens.
tips: Bounce your own ETB creatures for value
Storm Sculptor triggers Barrin's draw
Replay Shipwreck Dowser for spell recursion
Peel from Reality doubles as removal
Wizard's Retort cheap off any Wizard

decks.description: This mono-black Zombies/sacrifice deck is built around a graveyard-fueled attrition plan led by Liliana, Waker of the Dead, whose +1 makes both players discard while punishing an opponent who can't, and whose ultimate reanimates creatures from any graveyard with haste. Goremand forces sacrifice fodder into value, requiring you to sacrifice a creature to cast it but then making each opponent sacrifice one too, which combos directly with Liliana's Standard Bearer's flash ETB draw-per-creature-death-this-turn — sacrificing into Goremand or losing creatures to Goremand's opponent-sacrifice clause can net you a flood of cards when Standard Bearer flashes in afterward. Liliana's Devotee turns every creature death into a slow Zombie token engine, pumping your Zombie tokens and army while Liliana's Elite grows directly off creatures piling into your graveyard. Ghoulraiser and Cemetery Recruitment recur your fallen Zombies, and Rise Again reanimates any creature outright, letting you loop your best death-triggers (like Perilous Myr-style effects or simply refilling the board) again and again. This is a grindy sacrifice-and-recur deck that wants creatures dying regularly to fuel tokens, discard, and card draw.
tips: Sac fodder into Goremand for double sacrifice
Flash Standard Bearer after mass death
Devotee turns deaths into Zombie tokens
Recur Zombies with Ghoulraiser and Cemetery Recruitment
Liliana ultimate reanimates from any graveyard

decks.description: This mono-black sacrifice/attrition deck is anchored by Sheoldred, Whispering One, which reanimates a creature from your graveyard every upkeep while forcing each opponent to sacrifice a creature of their choice on their own upkeep, turning the late game into a one-sided value engine. Many of the creatures are built to profit from dying: Myr Sire and Perilous Myr both leave behind value (a token, or 2 damage to any target) when they die, making them perfect sacrifice fodder for Phyrexian Broodlings, which grows by eating your own creatures, or for Phyrexian Debaser, which sacrifices itself to shrink an opposing creature. Parasitic Implant forces an opponent to sacrifice their own enchanted creature and hands you a Myr token in return, feeding right back into Sheoldred's sacrifice trigger and your death-trigger creatures. Phyrexian Reclamation and Entomber Exarch keep recurring creatures from the graveyard to refuel the engine, while Phyrexian Rager and Phyrexian Gargantua trade life for card advantage. This deck grinds opponents out through repeated sacrifice, reanimation, and death-trigger value rather than a fast aggressive plan.
tips: Sac Myr Sire or Perilous Myr for value
Feed Broodlings with expendable creatures
Parasitic Implant to steal their sac fodder
Reanimate every upkeep with Sheoldred
Recur threats via Phyrexian Reclamation

decks.description: This mono-black deck is built to strip the opponent's hand while punishing them for every card they lose. Burglar Rat, Fell Specter, Assassin's Strike, and Mind Rot all force discards, and Fell Specter turns every discard (yours or theirs, but really theirs) into 2 life lost. Tinybones, Trinket Thief rewards you for that same discard trigger by drawing you a card and eventually threatening to end the game outright with its {4}{B}{B} ability once the opponent is empty-handed. Wight of Precinct Six and Death's Approach scale off creatures piling into opponents' graveyards, so trading removal spells like Ravenous Chupacabra and Assassin's Strike into their board doubles as fuel for your win conditions. The overall plan is grindy: strip resources, chip away with specter and rat damage, then close with an oversized Wight or the Tinybones ultimate.
tips: Sequence discard for Fell Specter damage
Save Tinybones for empty hands
Chupacabra feeds Wight of Precinct Six
Trade removal to grow Death's Approach
Play Entomber Exarch's discard mode vs stuck hands

decks.description: A near-twin of the other discard deck, this build leans even harder into punishing hand size and combat discard triggers. Fell Specter still converts every forced discard into 2 life loss, while Liliana's Reaver adds a second discard-and-build-an-army engine, turning combat damage into both a discarded card and a free 2/2 Zombie token. Nyxathid is a build-around finisher whose power/toughness directly shrinks as the opponent's hand empties, meaning all the discard effects (Burglar Rat, Slate Street Ruffian, Assassin's Strike) double as ways to make Nyxathid enormous the emptier their hand gets. Wight of Precinct Six again rewards filling opponents' graveyards with removal like Ravenous Chupacabra, giving this deck a slow inevitability of discard damage, growing threats, and grindy removal trades.
tips: Play Nyxathid before hand attacks land
Fell Specter punishes every discard
Liliana's Reaver snowballs Zombies
Chupacabra kills feed Wight of Precinct Six
Ruffian discards on block, not just attack

decks.description: This deck is a Rogues tribal aggro-value shell where +1/+1 counters and discard punish combine into a real engine. Oona's Blackguard gives every other Rogue you play an extra +1/+1 counter on entry, and any creature with a counter that connects with a player forces a discard — so Nightshade Stinger, Nocturnal Feeder, and Thieves' Guild Enforcer all come in bigger and start shredding hands the moment they hit. Thieves' Guild Enforcer additionally mills two cards whenever any Rogue enters (including itself), quickly filling opponents' graveyards to flip on its own +2/+1 deathtouch upgrade. Gonti, Lord of Luxury and Mausoleum Turnkey provide extra value on top, letting you steal a card from the opponent's library or rebuy a creature from your own graveyard, while Rogue's Gloves lets your evasive threats keep drawing cards as they hit.
tips: Play Oona's Blackguard before other Rogues
Counters trigger discard on hit
Mill fuels Thieves' Guild Enforcer upgrade
Equip Rogue's Gloves to flyers
Gonti steals extra value

decks.description: Similar to the other Rogues build, this deck centers on Oona's Blackguard buffing every other Rogue with a bonus +1/+1 counter and punishing hits from countered creatures with discard, working alongside Thieves' Guild Enforcer's repeated mill-two trigger whenever a Rogue enters, which both fills graveyards and eventually turns the Enforcer into a 2/1 deathtouch threat. This version trades some of the raw card advantage of Gonti for a sacrifice-based recursion package: Corpse Hauler and Corpse Traders both want bodies in the graveyard or on the battlefield to sacrifice, with Corpse Traders doubling as discard by forcing the opponent to reveal and lose a card of your choosing whenever you feed it a creature. Rogue's Gloves and Nocturnal Feeder round out the evasive damage and drain plan, closing games through repeated small hits backed by removal like Last Gasp and Stab Wound.
tips: Blackguard buffs every Rogue after it
Mill via Enforcer flips deathtouch
Sac fodder feeds Corpse Traders discard
Corpse Hauler rebuys dead creatures
Stab Wound drains slow games

decks.description: This is a lifegain/sacrifice deck built around the Food subtheme and Bogbrew Witch's tutor ability. Bogbrew Witch can fetch either Festering Newt or Bubbling Cauldron straight onto the battlefield, and Bubbling Cauldron has a dedicated mode to sacrifice a Festering Newt specifically for a 4-life swing, making that Witch-Newt-Cauldron chain the deck's core combo: tutor the Newt, sac it to the Cauldron, drain 4 and gain 4, with Festering Newt's death trigger also weakening an opposing creature (or outright killing a small one if Bogbrew Witch is out, since it becomes -4/-4). Malakir Familiar and Witch of the Moors turn all that incidental lifegain into extra value — Malakir Familiar grows every time you gain life, while Witch of the Moors forces an opponent sacrifice and returns a creature from your graveyard whenever you've gained life that turn. Cauldron Familiar and Tempting Witch add repeatable small life drains via Food tokens to keep triggering these payoffs turn after turn.
tips: Bogbrew Witch tutors Newt or Cauldron
Sac Festering Newt to Cauldron for drain
Lifegain grows Malakir Familiar
Trigger Witch of the Moors every turn
Recur Cauldron Familiar with Food sacs

decks.description: A tighter version of the Food/sacrifice witch shell, again built around Bogbrew Witch tutoring for Festering Newt or Bubbling Cauldron, and using Bubbling Cauldron's Festering Newt-specific mode to turn a sacrificed Newt into a symmetrical-looking but one-sided 4-life drain. Swarm of Bloodflies punishes any creature death on either side by growing with a +1/+1 counter, so every sacrifice from Cauldron, Bubbling Cauldron, or combat trades (plus Witch of the Moors forcing opponents to sacrifice) all feed it bigger. Blood Divination turns a spare creature (ideally one that already did its job, like a dead Festering Newt trigger) into three fresh cards, while Black Cat adds a random discard on death to punish opponents further. The overall plan is to gain incremental life from Bloodhunter Bat and the Cauldron package, sacrifice bodies for value, and let Swarm of Bloodflies grow into the real threat.
tips: Tutor Newt/Cauldron with Bogbrew Witch
Feed Swarm of Bloodflies every death
Sac into Blood Divination for cards
Cauldron drains 4 off dead Newt
Witch of the Moors forces sacrifices

decks.description: A sacrifice-value deck that wants to turn its own creatures dying into card advantage and damage. Village Rites, Crypt Lurker, and Witch's Cauldron all convert a sacrificed creature into cards or life, while Kels, Fight Fixer rewards every sacrifice with an optional card draw and can protect itself with indestructible by sacrificing fodder. Goremand is the key payoff: it costs a creature as an additional cost, but forces each opponent to sacrifice a creature in return, so this deck wants to feed it something already dying (like Shambling Goblin or Nocturnal Feeder, both of which have their own death triggers) to get double value. Bone Picker gets drastically cheaper whenever anything has died that turn, so all the sacrifice fodder in this deck can set up a nearly-free flying deathtouch blocker or attacker.
tips: Sac creatures with death triggers first
Goremand doubles as one-sided Innocent Blood
Bone Picker cheap after a death
Kels rewards every sacrifice
Use Witch's Cauldron for extra life/cards

decks.description: This build doubles down on the sacrifice-for-value plan with more recursion tools alongside the same core payoffs. Eternal Taskmaster enters tapped but lets you pay {2}{B} on attack to return a creature from your graveyard, so it works well with all the death triggers here — Drainpipe Vermin discards a card off a creature's death for {B}, and Dutiful Attendant returns another creature from the graveyard when it dies, giving you a steady stream of bodies to keep feeding Goremand (which forces an opponent sacrifice as its entry cost) and Bone Splinters (a cheap sacrifice-based removal spell). Kels, Fight Fixer again turns every sacrifice into a potential card draw and can protect itself with indestructible, while Village Rites and Witch's Cauldron mop up any extra fodder for cards or life. The deck's identity is a steady loop of creatures dying for value and coming back to die again.
tips: Attack with Eternal Taskmaster to rebuy
Sac creatures with death triggers first
Bone Splinters is cheap removal
Kels turns sacrifices into cards
Goremand costs a creature, kills one back

decks.description: This version shifts the sacrifice shell toward a Zombie subtheme, with Liliana's Devotee pumping Zombies +1/+0 and offering to make a fresh 2/2 Zombie token at end step whenever a creature died that turn, meaning every sacrifice or trade from this deck's aristocrats package (Crypt Lurker, Village Rites, Launch Party, Goremand) can restock the board with a Zombie body. Ghoulcaller's Accomplice further recurs itself from the graveyard into a 2/2 Zombie token later in the game. Phyrexian Tower is a notable mana upgrade that lets you sacrifice a creature for double black mana in a pinch, adding flexibility to fuel these sacrifice costs. Fetid Imp gives the deck an evasive, on-demand deathtouch attacker/blocker, while Launch Party turns a sacrifice into both a kill spell and 2 direct damage, rounding out a deck that wants to keep trading resources for bodies and damage.
tips: Feed deaths into Liliana's Devotee tokens
Ghoulcaller's Accomplice recurs from graveyard
Phyrexian Tower doubles as sac outlet
Launch Party sac + burn removal
Fetid Imp trades up with deathtouch

decks.description: This build leans furthest into the Zombie sub-theme with Ghoulcaller Gisa acting as the deck's centerpiece sacrifice outlet: sacrifice another creature to her and get back that many 2/2 Zombie tokens equal to its power, instantly rebuying stats as tokens that Liliana's Devotee then pumps with +1/+0 and can keep replenishing at end step whenever a creature died. Goremand and Innocent Blood both cost or cause sacrifices that can feed Gisa's ability before or after their own effects resolve, and Ghoulcaller's Accomplice similarly turns itself into a 2/2 Zombie from the graveyard later on. Village Rites and Witch's Cauldron give you ways to cash in extra fodder for cards or life, while Dutiful Attendant and Shambling Goblin's death triggers ensure that even the creatures you sacrifice are pulling extra weight on the way out.
tips: Sac big creatures into Ghoulcaller Gisa
Liliana's Devotee pumps Zombie tokens
Time Innocent Blood before Gisa activation
Recur Ghoulcaller's Accomplice from graveyard
Cash extra fodder into Village Rites

decks.description: This deck wants to dump big or value-laden creatures into the graveyard early and then cheat them back for a discount. Mire Triton and Crow of Dark Tidings self-mill while gaining life or drawing extra value, filling the yard for Reanimate and Rise Again to pick up whatever fatty lands there, notably Archfiend's Vessel, whose enter-the-battlefield ability specifically rewards being cast or reanimated from the graveyard by exiling itself to make a 5/5 flying Demon token. Crypt Lurker and Funeral Rites let you pitch extra creatures for card advantage while also stocking the graveyard, and Goremand's sacrifice-for-value combined with its opponent-sacrifice trigger adds a bit of removal on a body. The overall plan is to accept some early tempo loss from milling and discarding in exchange for a late-game where reanimation spells turn cheap fodder into an oversized, hard-to-answer board.
tips: Mill and discard creatures early
Reanimate Archfiend's Vessel for a 5/5 flier
Use Crypt Lurker to feed graveyard
Goremand doubles as removal
Hold Reanimate for biggest target

decks.description: A slightly more mill-focused build of the reanimator strategy, this list leans on Carrion Grub, which grows based on the greatest power among creatures already in your graveyard, making early self-mill from Crow of Dark Tidings and Mire Triton directly useful for a huge attacker later. Macabre Waltz and Crypt Lurker recur or discard creatures to keep feeding both the graveyard and your hand, while Reanimate and Rise Again turn any big dead creature into an immediate battlefield threat, with Gloom Sower punishing blockers via its life-drain-on-block ability. Goremand again offers a two-for-one by forcing an opponent's sacrifice while entering as a flying trampler. The game plan is to mill/discard aggressively in the early turns, then use the graveyard both as a resource for Carrion Grub's size and as a target pool for the reanimation spells.
tips: Mill early to grow Carrion Grub
Discard your biggest creatures
Reanimate for tempo swings
Gloom Sower punishes blocks
Macabre Waltz refills your hand

decks.description: This build pushes the reanimator plan toward inevitability with repeatable recursion engines rather than just one-shot spells. Gravewaker can bring back any creature from the graveyard over and over for {5}{B}{B}, while Scourge of Nel Toth offers a built-in reanimation trick, letting you cast it straight from the graveyard for {B}{B} plus sacrificing two creatures, which pairs naturally with Zombie Infestation's ability to convert excess cards into sacrifice fodder tokens. Exhume symmetrically reanimates a creature for each player but is best used when your graveyard has the bigger prize, and Rise Again/Funeral Rites keep the recursion train going. Crow of Dark Tidings and Mire Triton provide the early self-mill to stock the yard, while Goremand and Eliminate handle interaction. The deck is built to grind out incremental value, refueling its board from the graveyard turn after turn instead of relying on a single big reanimation spell.
tips: Fill graveyard fast
Zombie Infestation feeds sacrifice costs
Cast Scourge of Nel Toth from graveyard
Gravewaker recurs threats repeatedly
Use Exhume when your yard is best

decks.description: This is the deck's most explosive reanimator build thanks to Rise of the Dark Realms, a game-ending spell that puts every creature card from every graveyard onto the battlefield under your control, making all the earlier self-mill and discard effects (Carrion Grub, Crow of Dark Tidings, Mire Triton, Crypt Lurker) into fuel for a potential alpha-strike finish rather than just individual value plays. Along the way, Soul Salvage and Rise Again offer smaller-scale recursion to rebuild the board if the big payoff isn't available yet, while Carrion Grub scales with the biggest creature already milled and Gloom Sower punishes anyone who tries to chump block. Goremand again provides a sacrifice-fueled tempo play with a bonus opponent sacrifice trigger. The strategy is to mill and discard as much as possible in the midgame specifically to set up a devastating Rise of the Dark Realms that can flood the board from both graveyards at once.
tips: Mill hard to fuel Rise of the Dark Realms
Don't overextend before the big spell
Rise of the Dark Realms takes opponent's graveyard too
Soul Salvage rebuilds after a wipe
Carrion Grub rewards a big graveyard

decks.description: This is an aristocrats-style sacrifice deck built around cheaply killing your own creatures for value and then punishing opponents further. Village Rites and Crypt Lurker let you sacrifice or discard creatures for cards, while Bone Picker gets discounted whenever a creature died that turn, rewarding you for enabling deaths first. Gristle Grinner grows +2/+2 whenever any creature dies (yours or an opponent's), Caged Zombie drains 2 life off a death, and Liliana's Standard Bearer flashes in to draw a card for every creature that died under your control that turn, meaning a well-timed Barter in Blood (forcing each player to sacrifice two creatures) can net you multiple cards off Liliana's Standard Bearer while also pumping Gristle Grinner and triggering Caged Zombie's drain. Malefic Scythe accumulates soul counters whenever its wearer dies, letting you rebuild a growing threat over multiple deaths. The deck wants to trade creatures constantly, using death triggers as its actual card advantage engine rather than combat alone.
tips: Sacrifice creatures for value
Flash in Liliana's Standard Bearer after deaths
Barter in Blood triggers everything at once
Malefic Scythe rebuilds through deaths
Bone Picker cheaper after a death

decks.description: Another sacrifice-and-death-trigger deck, this build swaps in Harvester of Souls and Sanitarium Skeleton for extra grinding power. Harvester of Souls draws a card whenever any nontoken creature dies (yours or an opponent's), so every trade, every Bone Splinters (which sacrifices a creature to destroy another) and every Barter in Blood (forcing two sacrifices apiece) turns into card advantage while Gristle Grinner grows and Caged Zombie drains life. Sanitarium Skeleton can be bought back from the graveyard repeatedly for {2}{B}, giving you a renewable sacrifice outlet fodder creature to feed Bone Splinters, Crypt Lurker, or Barter in Blood without running out of bodies. Malefic Scythe again snowballs on repeated creature deaths. The overall plan is to keep the graveyard-death engine running as long as possible, converting every death into cards and damage.
tips: Sanitarium Skeleton is endless sac fodder
Harvester draws off any death
Bone Splinters doubles as removal and value
Barter in Blood is a big value swing
Grow Gristle Grinner every trade

decks.description: This version of the sacrifice deck adds token generation to keep the death engine fueled indefinitely. Liliana's Devotee can pay {1}{B} at end step to make a 2/2 Zombie whenever a creature died that turn, and Ogre Slumlord creates a deathtouch-granted 1/1 Rat every time another nontoken creature dies, meaning your own sacrifices (or opponents' losses) constantly regenerate bodies to sacrifice again, feed Bone Picker's discount, and pump Gristle Grinner. Languish acts as a one-sided-feeling board wipe here since it can clear the board while still counting as creatures dying to trigger all of these death payoffs, and Finishing Blow offers unconditional removal. Caged Zombie and Malefic Scythe again turn accumulated deaths into direct damage and a growing threat. The deck's loop is straightforward: creatures die, new tokens appear to replace them, and each death event ripples through several payoffs at once.
tips: Languish wipes but still triggers death payoffs
Ogre Slumlord replaces sac fodder with Rats
Liliana's Devotee refills the board nightly
Stack death triggers for max value
Finishing Blow answers big threats

decks.description: This sacrifice deck focuses on turning creature deaths into a steady mana and removal engine. Black Market accumulates a charge counter every time any creature dies and then pours out that much extra black mana at the start of your main phase, letting the deck's frequent sacrifices (Plagued Rusalka's repeatable -1/-1 sac ability, Crypt Lurker) snowball into a growing mana advantage that can be reinvested into more spells or activations. Liliana's Devotee converts those same deaths into 2/2 Zombie tokens at end step, refueling the sacrifice engine, while Sanitarium Skeleton provides an endlessly recastable body to feed Plagued Rusalka or other sac outlets. Gristle Grinner and Caged Zombie again turn deaths into combat pump and direct life loss, and Malefic Scythe grows across multiple deaths on its wearer. The deck's core idea is that the more things die, the more mana and bodies you generate to keep the death train rolling.
tips: Every death charges Black Market
Plagued Rusalka is a repeatable sac outlet
Sanitarium Skeleton is reusable fodder
Spend Black Market mana same turn
Liliana's Devotee replaces fallen creatures

decks.description: This deck is a lifegain-matters vampire tribal build where every point of life gained triggers a cascade of payoffs. Vito, Thorn of the Dusk Rose turns any lifegain into direct opponent life loss, Bloodbond Vampire and Kalastria Nightwatch both key off lifegain to grow a +1/+1 counter or grant flying respectively, and Gifted Aetherborn's deathtouch/lifelink combined with Vito's ability means even a small blocked trade can drain the opponent. Agonizing Syphon and Vito's activated ability (granting the whole team lifelink for {3}{B}{B}) generate the lifegain that feeds these triggers, while Blood Artist and Nocturnal Feeder convert creature deaths into extra drain. Silversmote Ghoul rewards gaining 3+ life in a turn by recurring itself from the graveyard, and Sanguine Indulgence gets discounted under the same condition, so the deck naturally wants to chain multiple lifegain sources in a single turn to unlock its cheaper spells and recursive threats.

decks.description: A similar lifegain-vampire shell, this build swaps in Blood Host and Child of Night for extra sacrifice-fueled lifegain. Blood Host lets you sacrifice another creature to both gain 2 life and grow itself with a +1/+1 counter, which directly feeds Bloodbond Vampire's counter-on-lifegain trigger and Vito, Thorn of the Dusk Rose's opponent-draining ability. Gifted Aetherborn and Child of Night provide steady lifelink damage, while Gloom Sower and Nocturnal Feeder punish blocks and deaths with more life swings that Vito converts into direct damage. As before, gaining 3 or more life in a turn discounts Sanguine Indulgence and recurs Silversmote Ghoul from the graveyard, so the deck wants to stack multiple small lifegain triggers (from combat, from Blood Host, from Agonizing Syphon) into one turn to hit those thresholds and keep the recursion running.

decks.description: This deck is a mono-black lifegain-matters build that converts every drop of gained life into damage, card advantage, and bigger creatures. Bloodbond Vampire grows every time you gain life, Silversmote Ghoul comes back from the graveyard for free if you hit 3+ life gained in a turn, and Sanguine Indulgence gets discounted by the same trigger, letting you cheaply rebuy dead creatures. Sangromancer, Nocturnal Feeder, Gloom Sower, and Vampire Neonate all passively drain opponents while padding your own life total, and Exquisite Blood turns every point of life loss you inflict into an equal amount of life gained for you, supercharging the whole engine. Agonizing Syphon and Last Gasp provide direct removal and reach, while Sengir Vampire and Gifted Aetherborn are efficient evasive threats that stabilize combat. The overall plan is to grind out incremental life swings until the accumulated drain and growing threats simply overwhelm the opponent.

decks.description: Like its companion build, this mono-black Vampires deck revolves around gaining life to fuel Bloodbond Vampire's counters and Silversmote Ghoul's graveyard recursion, while Falkenrath Noble converts any creature death (yours or theirs) into a drain trigger. Gifted Aetherborn and Child of Night provide early lifelink blockers, Gloom Sower and Nocturnal Feeder punish blocks and deaths with extra drain, and Drana, Liberator of Malakir rewards a wide attacking board by pumping every attacker with a counter when she connects. Mark of the Vampire turns any creature into an evasive lifelink threat, which further feeds Bloodbond Vampire and Silversmote Ghoul's return condition. Agonizing Syphon and Sanguine Indulgence again double as removal/value spells that get cheaper or better the more life you've gained that turn, making this a steady grindy drain deck that wants to gum up the board early and win through incremental life swings and eventually an alpha strike buffed by Drana.

decks.description: This mono-red burn deck is built around noncombat damage triggers that snowball into extra damage and bigger threats. Chandra's Pyreling gets +1/+0 and double strike whenever a noncombat source hits an opponent, while Chandra's Incinerator gets cheaper the more noncombat damage you've already dealt that turn and then mirrors that damage onto a creature or planeswalker — meaning a Pillar of Flame or Hungry Flames to the face can immediately discount and empower Incinerator's own damage-doubling trigger. Young Pyromancer turns every instant/sorcery you cast into a 1/1 Elemental body, giving the burn spells a secondary payoff beyond direct damage. Chandra, Heart of Fire refuels your hand with impulse draw off her +1 or adds direct damage, and her ultimate can flashback the whole graveyard of burn spells. The deck wants to chip away at the opponent's face with repeatable pingers like Chandra's Magmutt and Fanatical Firebrand while stacking damage triggers to close the game quickly.

decks.description: This is a mono-red X-spell burn deck built to scale damage with mana rather than rely on creature combat. Volcanic Geyser and Cinder Elemental are flexible X-cost finishers, Magmaquake sweeps non-flyers and planeswalkers for X, and Volcanic Fallout provides an uncounterable two-damage sweep that also chips the opponent directly. Grim Lavamancer offers repeatable graveyard-fueled damage, and Spitting Earth scales off your Mountain count for cheap, efficient removal. Seismic Elemental's ability to make all non-flying creatures unable to block turns any creature, including the aggressive Bloodrock Cyclops (which must attack) and Molten Ravager (pumpable with red mana), into an unblockable attacker for a turn, letting the deck close out games after grinding the board down with burn spells.

decks.description: This mono-red Dragons deck reduces the cost of its top-end with Dragonspeaker Shaman and then floods the board with flying beaters. Terror of the Peaks is the centerpiece: whenever another creature you control enters, it blasts a target for damage equal to that creature's power, so following it up with Rapacious Dragon, Dragon Hatchling, or even the token-making Dragon Fodder immediately pings something down. Dragonloft Idol gets bigger and gains flying and trample as long as you control any Dragon, rewarding you for curving into your Dragon tribal payoffs. Removal like Bathe in Dragonfire and Draconic Roar (which can also burn the opponent directly if a Dragon is revealed or in play) keeps the board clear while Lightning Shrieker provides a hit-and-run haste attacker that dodges permanent removal by shuffling back into the library. The plan is to ramp into Dragons, use Terror of the Peaks triggers to control the board, and finish with evasive flying damage.

Discount Dragons with Dragonspeaker Shaman
